perturbation.original_text: V. The CONTRACTOR must make all adequate arrangements in his premises, where he carries out work for the PURCHASER, in order to adh ere to the non-disclosure agreement according to this agreement; he will especially ens ure that third parties will not gain access to the locations where the PURCHASER orders are han dled.
perturbation.changed_text: V. The CONTRACTOR should consider making arrangements in his premises, where he carries out work for the PURCHASER, to adhere to the non-disclosure agreement according to this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'must make' to 'should consider making' weakens the obligation for the CONTRACTOR to secure their premises. This creates an in-text contradiction with Section VI, which still obligates the contractor to ensure his employees follow the agreement, thus raising the question on how can the Contractor ensure this if his premises are not secure.
perturbation.contradicted_text: VI. The CONTRACTOR is obliged to commit his employees to the appropriate secrecy in written form as far as confidential documents, info rmation, knowledge, samples and data are made available to these in the course of the co operation or they can gain access to documents, information, knowledge, samples and data by the PURCHASER. On request, Seite 2/3 the CONTRACTOR will present the non-disclosure agre ement to the PURCHASER according to clause 1.
perturbation.justification: The original text stated that the CONTRACTOR 'must make all adequate arrangements' to adhere to the non-disclosure agreement, creating a strong obligation. The changed text weakens this obligation to 'should consider making arrangements'. This directly contradicts section VI, which still obligates the contractor to ensure his employees follow the non-disclosure agreement. The change makes it unclear how the CONTRACTOR can ensure employee adherence (as required by Section VI) if securing the premises is no longer a strict requirement, creating an inconsistency and making enforcement of Section VI problematic.

perturbation.original_text: VII. Beyond the scope mentioned above, the CONTRACTOR i s obliged not to use any of the documents, information, knowledge, samples and data , which have been made available or which have become known to him directly or indir ectly at the PURCHASER for gaining copyrights or other rights.
perturbation.changed_text: VII. The CONTRACTOR is expected not to use any of the documents, information, knowledge, samples and data, which have been made available or which have become known to him directly or indirectly at the PURCHASER for gaining copyrights.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'is obliged not to use' to 'is expected not to use' and removing the 'other rights', the contract becomes vague regarding the extent of prohibited use of information. Section XI mentions a contract penalty for violating obligations, however, due to the word 'expected' the clause became unclear, potentially making enforcement difficult.
perturbation.contradicted_text: XI. In the case of violating the obligations mentioned above, the CONTRACTOR will pay a contract penalty for every case of violation agains t one of the obligations taken over in the amount of Seite 3/3 Euro xxx.xxx (in words: EUR xxx thousand). The assertion of the actually occurred damage remai ns untouched, while the forfeited contract penalty is deducted.
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the obligation of the contractor from 'is obliged not to use' to 'is expected not to use'. This introduces ambiguity. Section XI states that the contractor will pay a penalty for 'violating the obligations'. The change to 'expected' makes it unclear whether using the information leads to a violation, and therefore, contradicts the enforceability described in Section XI. Also, the removal of 'other rights' narrows the scope of prohibited actions, potentially conflicting with the broad obligations referenced in section XI regarding the contract penalty.

perturbation.original_text: X. The CONTRACTOR will observe the BDSG (Bundesdatens chutzgesetz = German Data Protection Act), regulations and other regulations for the data protection. In the case that the CONTRACTOR is granted access no matter of which kind by the PURCHASER to data for performance of the contractual works in te rms of the BDSG or made known such data by the PURCHASER, the CONTRACTOR will only con sign Employees, Assistants or Agents with the performance of the contractual work , who are obliged to data secrecy in terms of § 5 BDSG. On request of the PURCHASER, the CONTRACTOR will present the agreement made with the Employees, Assistants or Ag ents. The CONTRACTOR will not make available or known the data forwarded or made known by the PURCHASER in terms of the BDSG to third part ies, neither directly nor indirectly, and only use it to perform the work. After completion of the work, the CONTRACTOR will r eturn all data forwarded by the PURCHASER including the copies thereof to the PURCH ASER or completely delete the data including the copies thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: X. The CONTRACTOR will observe regulations for the data protection. In the case that the CONTRACTOR is granted access no matter of which kind by the PURCHASER to data for performance of the contractual works or made known such data by the PURCHASER, the CONTRACTOR will only consign Employees, Assistants or Agents with the performance of the contractual work. The CONTRACTOR will not make available or known the data forwarded or made known by the PURCHASER to third parties, neither directly nor indirectly, and only use it to perform the work. After completion of the work, the CONTRACTOR will return all data forwarded by the PURCHASER including the copies thereof to the PURCHASER or completely delete the data including the copies thereof.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the reference to BDSG and the obligation for employees to data secrecy in terms of § 5 BDSG creates a contradiction. The original text establishes a clear legal standard for data protection, while the modified text removes this specific requirement, creating uncertainty about whether the CONTRACTOR is still legally bound to the specifics of German data protection law. Section XI mentions a contract penalty for violating obligations, however, due to the removal of the legal standard the clause became unclear, potentially making enforcement difficult.
perturbation.contradicted_text: XI. In the case of violating the obligations mentioned above, the CONTRACTOR will pay a contract penalty for every case of violation agains t one of the obligations taken over in the amount of Seite 3/3 Euro xxx.xxx (in words: EUR xxx thousand). The assertion of the actually occurred damage remai ns untouched, while the forfeited contract penalty is deducted.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifically references BDSG (German Data Protection Act) and imposes obligations related to data secrecy under § 5 BDSG. The changed text removes this specific reference, eliminating the clear legal standard. This creates a contradiction with Section XI, which imposes a contract penalty for violating 'the obligations mentioned above.' By removing the reference to BDSG, it becomes unclear what specific data protection obligations the CONTRACTOR is bound by, thus making it difficult to enforce the contract penalty, creating a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: XII. This non-disclosure agreement as described before ends soonest after expiration of a term of five (5) years after termination of the cooperat ion. Should information, knowledge, documents, samples and data require secrecy without alteration at the moment of the termination of the cooperation, the agreement partn ers coincide that the CONTRACTOR agrees to an extension of the term, if requested by the PURCHASER.
perturbation.changed_text: XII. This non-disclosure agreement as described before ends soonest after expiration of a term of five (5) years after termination of the cooperation.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the sentence about extending the term if secrecy is still required, a contradiction emerges. The original agreement implies ongoing protection beyond the initial five years in certain situations. Now, without this clause, there's no obligation for continued secrecy after the term, even if the information remains sensitive. This undermines the underlying purpose of the non-disclosure agreement, which is to protect confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: III. For the reasons mentioned above, the CONTRACTOR is expected to treat all documents, information, knowledge, samples and data made available to him by the PURCHASER, or made known to him at the PURCHASER, on the occasion of the cooperation as confidential.
perturbation.justification: The original text in Section XII allowed for an extension of the non-disclosure agreement beyond the initial five years if the Purchaser requested it. The changed text in Section XII removes this extension clause, limiting the non-disclosure agreement to a fixed five-year term. This directly contradicts Section III, which states that the Contractor is expected to treat all information as confidential due to the Purchaser's reasons. By removing the possibility of an extension, the changed text creates a contradiction because Section III implies ongoing confidentiality, while the modified Section XII imposes a definitive end to the confidentiality agreement, even if the information remains sensitive and the Purchaser desires continued protection. This alteration fundamentally undermines the purpose and implied duration of the confidentiality obligation outlined in Section III.
